The first words of this chapter seem like an answer to the mockers of those days. Here is a prophecy about John the Baptist’s appearance. He is Christ's forerunner. He will prepare the way before him by calling people to repentance. The Messiah had been long called, “He that should come,” and now he will shortly come. He is the Messenger of the covenant. Those who seek Jesus will find pleasure in him, often when not expected. The Lord Jesus prepares the sinner's heart to be his temple through the ministry of his word and the convictions of his Spirit, and he enters it as the Messenger of peace and consolation. No hypocrite or formalist can endure his teaching or stand before his judgment seat. Christ came to distinguish people, to separate between the valuable and the worthless. He will sit as a Refiner. Christ, through his gospel, will purify and reform his church, and by his Spirit working with it, will regenerate and cleanse souls. He will remove the impurities found in them. He will separate their corruptions, which make their abilities worthless and useless. The believer need not fear the intense trial of afflictions and temptations by which the Savior refines his gold. He will see to it that it is not more intense or longer than is needed for his good; and this trial will end very differently from that of the wicked. Christ will, through interceding for them, make them accepted. Where there is no fear of God, no good can be expected. Evil pursues sinners. God is unchanging. And though the sentence against evil works is not executed quickly, it will be executed; the Lord is as much an enemy to sin as ever. We may all apply this to ourselves. Because we have to do with a God that does not change, therefore we are not consumed; because his compassion fails not.

The people of that generation turned away from God; they had not kept his commands. God gives them a gracious invitation. But they said, Wherein shall we return? God notices how our hearts respond to the invitations of his word. It shows great perverseness in sin when people make afflictions excuses for sin, which are sent to separate them from their sins. Here is an earnest encouragement to reform. God must be served first; and the interest of our souls ought to be preferred before that of our bodies. Let them trust God to provide for their comfort. God has blessings ready for us, but through the weakness of our faith and the narrowness of our desires, we have not room to receive them. He who makes trial will find nothing is lost by honoring the Lord with his possessions.

Among the Jews at this time, some plainly revealed themselves to be children of the wicked one. The yoke of Christ is easy. But those who work wickedness tempt God through presumptuous sins. Judge of things as they will appear when the doom of these proud sinners comes to be executed. Those that feared the Lord spoke kindly, for preserving and promoting mutual love, when sin thus abounded. They spoke one to another in the language of those that fear the Lord and think on his name. As evil communications corrupt good minds and manners, so good communications confirm them. A book of remembrance was written before God. He will see to it that his children do not perish with those who do not believe. They shall be vessels of mercy and honor when the rest are made vessels of wrath and dishonor. The saints are God’s jewels; they are dear to him. He will preserve them as his jewels when the earth is burned up like dross. Those who now claim God for theirs, he will then claim for his. It is our duty to serve God with the disposition of children; and he will not have his children raised up in idleness; they must do him service from a principle of love. Even God’s children stand in need of sparing mercy. All are righteous or wicked, such as serve God, or such as serve him not: all are going to heaven or to hell. We are often deceived in our opinions concerning both the one and the other; but at the bar of Christ, every man's character will be known. As to ourselves, we have need to think among which we shall have our lot; and, as to others, we must judge nothing before the time. But in the end all the world will confess that those alone were wise and happy who served the Lord and trusted in Him.
